title: make the operation status more detailed
body:
### Feature request description

Currently, when I run a command in Podman—no matter what happens—it just returns the container name, so I can’t tell if the operation was successful or not. It doesn’t give an error code or any other information—just the container name. What exactly am I supposed to do with the container name if I was the one who typed “podman rm ctr”?

```bash
$ podman rm ctr # ctr doesn't exist
ctr
```

### Suggest potential solution

In addition to the container name, you should include a description of what happened, such as "error: container 'ctr' was not deleted" or "ok: container 'ctr' successfully deleted," for each operation.

### Have you considered any alternatives?

I don't think there are any alternatives to adding verbosity

### Additional context

Nothing

comments.body:
I don't know if it's just me or if it hasn't been implemented yet.
In any case, it's very frustrating it doesn't make it clear what's going on.

> Currently, when I run a command in Podman—no matter what happens—it just returns the container name, so I can’t tell if the operation was successful or not.

That is just not true, cli commands use the exit code to signal error 0 means no error while anything else means an error happened. And also error messages get printed to stderr.
```
$ podman rm test
test
$ echo $?
0
$ podman rm test
Error: no container with ID or name "test" found: no such container
$ echo $?
1
```

It is pretty standard cli behaviour unless something went wrong there is no need to print extra into the clutters the terminal. The output is exactly what we did, when you type podman rm it means that container was removed

The name of the container only appears when it's removed, an error is properly thrown if the container does not exist.

```
$ ./bin/podman rm notexists
Error: no container with ID or name "notexists" found: no such container
$ ./bin/podman run --name exists alpine
$ ./bin/podman rm exists
exists
```
Closing as this is an established pattern. Please re-open if you think I've made a mistake.
